Nanyang Primary School

Primary 6 Science — Weighted Assessment 1 (2025)

Booklet A: 28 multiple-choice questions (56 marks) · Booklet B: 12 open-ended questions (44 marks) · Total 100 marks

Booklet A — Multiple Choice (56 marks)

Question 1

2 marks

Which of the following describe(s) both the field habitat and the garden habitat correctly?

A   Both habitats can have some common animal populations living in them.
B   The plants in both habitats provide food and shelter for some of the animals living there.
C   Flowering plants can only be found in the garden habitat while non-flowering plants can only be found in the field habitat.

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 1. Both a field and a garden can share common animal populations (A), and the plants in both provide food and shelter (B). Statement C is false because both flowering and non-flowering plants can grow in either habitat, so the answer is A and B only.

Question 2

2 marks

Nora saw bird X in a garden. Thirty minutes later, she concluded that bird X forms a population in the garden community.

Which of the following observations allowed her to make the conclusion?

A   Bird X eating earthworms
B   Bird X feeding two of its young
C   Bird X preyed on by its predator
D   Bird X building its nest with its mate

  1. A and B only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 3. A population is a group of the same kind of organism that can reproduce. Feeding its young (B) and building a nest with its mate (D) both show bird X reproducing with others of its kind, so more than one bird X exists. Eating earthworms (A) or being preyed upon (C) involve other organisms and do not show that bird X forms a population.

Question 3

2 marks

Study the food web below and answer questions 3 and 4.

Which of the following statements about the food web is false?

  1. Energy in organism E is transferred to organism D.
  2. Energy in organism B cannot be transferred to organism C.
  3. Energy in organism A is indirectly transferred to organism D.
  4. Organisms A, B, C, D and E belong to the same community.

Answer

Option 1. In the food web the arrows go A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D, D→E. Energy flows from D to E, not from E to D, so option 1 is false. (Option 2 is true: B and C are not linked; option 3 is true: A→B→D; option 4 is true: all live together in the same community.)

Question 4

2 marks

Which of the following changes in population size could be observed after an increase in organism E's population size?

  1. Increase in organism A's population size
  2. Increase in organism B's population size
  3. Increase in organism D's population size
  4. Decrease in organism C's population size

Answer

Option 2. E feeds on D, so more E means D decreases. With fewer D, fewer of B and C (D's food sources) are eaten, so B and C increase. Of the choices, the increase in organism B's population size is correct.

Question 5

2 marks

Which of the food webs below shows three populations of predators?

(1)
(2)
(3)
(4)

Answer

Option 4. A predator is an animal that eats another animal. The food web in option 4 has three organisms that each feed on another animal (three predators). The other webs have fewer animals acting as predators.

Question 6

2 marks

Which of the following examples of adaptations had been grouped correctly?

The correct grouping is option (1):

StructuralBehavioural
sharp clawssmall and light body
long tail finswebbed feet
body patternsproducing distinct sounds
hunting in groupsusing venom to kill prey

Answer

Option 3. Structural adaptations are physical body features (sharp claws, long tail fins, body patterns, webbed feet, light body) while behavioural adaptations are actions (hunting in groups, producing distinct sounds, using venom). Option 3 is the row that correctly separates physical features from behaviours.

Question 7

2 marks

Which of the following adaptations of plants is correctly matched with its purpose? The correct match is option (3):

AdaptationPurpose
Needle-like leaves of a cactusTo store water for the plant
Male reproductive parts that hang out of a flowerTo trap pollen in the air easily
Spongy leaf stalks filled with airTo allow the plant to float on water
Fruits of a plant that are brightly-colouredTo attract pollinators

Answer

Option 3. Spongy, air-filled leaf stalks make a water plant buoyant so it can float on water — a correctly matched adaptation and purpose. (Needle leaves reduce water loss, not store water; hanging stamens release pollen; brightly-coloured fruits attract seed dispersers, not pollinators.)

Question 8

2 marks

Which of the following describes adaptations of animals that prevent their predators from spotting them?

A   Changing body colour to green when on a green leaf
B   Changing fur colour from brown to white during snowy winter
C   A non-poisonous animal having the appearance of a poisonous animal
D   Having body shapes and colour that look like branches on the plant that they live on

  1. A only
  2. B and C only
  3. A, B and D only
  4. A, C and D only

Answer

Option 3. Camouflage hides an animal from predators: blending with a green leaf (A), turning white in snow (B), and looking like branches (D) all prevent the animal from being spotted. Mimicking a poisonous animal (C) is a warning to predators that the animal is dangerous, not a way to avoid being seen, so the answer is A, B and D only.

Question 9

2 marks

Which of the following is/are way(s) that help to conserve natural resources?

A   Use more paper bags instead of plastic bags
B   Using public transport instead of private vehicles
C   Clear forests by burning trees to make the soil fertile
D   Recycle used materials and make them into new products

  1. A and C only
  2. B and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. A, C and D only

Answer

Option 3. Using public transport (B) saves fuel and recycling (D) reuses materials — both conserve resources. Using more paper bags (A) uses more trees, and burning forests (C) destroys resources, so neither conserves. The answer is B and D only.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagram below shows a river. The arrow indicates the flow of river water.

The graph below shows the number of fish over time at part Y of the river.

Which of the following could have happened at part X of the river bank to cause the change in the number of fish?

A   Picking up litter
B   Planting more trees
C   Discharge of factory waste into the river
D   Spraying of pesticides regularly on plants growing by the river

  1. A and B only
  2. A and D only
  3. B and C only
  4. C and D only

Answer

Option 4. The number of fish at Y decreased. Since water flows from X to Y, pollution at X would be carried downstream. Discharging factory waste (C) and spraying pesticides (D) both pollute the water and harm/kill the fish. Picking up litter (A) and planting trees (B) would help, not harm, the fish.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagram below shows a flower that has developed into a fruit. Part X can germinate into a new plant.

Which statement(s) is/are correct?

A   Pollination had taken place.
B   Part X developed from an ovule.
C   The flower has many ovules in the ovary.

  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. A and C only
  4. B and C only

Answer

Option 2. For a flower to become a fruit, pollination then fertilisation must occur (A is correct). Part X is the seed, which develops from an ovule (B is correct). The fruit shown contains only one seed, so the ovary had one ovule, not many (C is false). The answer is A and B only.

Question 12

2 marks

The diagram below shows cells P and Q during process H in the reproduction of an animal.

Which one of the following correctly identifies cell P, cell Q and process H?

Cell PCell QProcess H
(1)eggspermfertilisation
(2)eggspermpollination
(3)spermeggfertilisation
(4)spermeggpollination

Answer

Option 1. The large round cell P is the egg and the smaller tailed cells Q are sperm. When a sperm joins with an egg, the process is fertilisation. Pollination applies to plants, not animals. So P = egg, Q = sperm, H = fertilisation.

Question 13

2 marks

Amy wanted to find out how the temperature of water affects the rate of movement of water in stems. She used three similar pieces of stem and three identical beakers with the same volume of water. She recorded the volume of water left in the beakers after 3 hours.

Which one of the following variables should be kept constant in her experiment?

  1. length of stem
  2. temperature of water
  3. volume of water transported up each piece of stem
  4. time taken for the water to reach the top of the stem

Answer

Option 1. The variable being changed is the water temperature, and the variable measured is the volume of water moved. To make the test fair, everything else — including the length of stem — must be kept the same. Temperature is the changed variable, while options 3 and 4 are measured results.

Question 14

2 marks

Read the information below and use it to answer questions 14 and 15.

Tom has a toy shown below. When he turns the handle, the rubber band becomes twisted. The wings move up and down loudly when the handle is released.

Tom turned the handle 40 times and threw the toy in the air at point P. The toy flew from P to Q, then flew at the same height from Q to R before stopping at S as shown below.

Which one of the following correctly shows the main energy changes in the toy before it is released at point P and when it flew to point P to Q?

Before release at PFrom P to Q
(1)kinetic energy →kinetic energy + sound energy
(2)kinetic energy →kinetic energy + potential energy + sound energy
(3)potential energy →kinetic energy + sound energy
(4)potential energy →kinetic energy + potential energy + sound energy

Answer

Option 4. Before release the twisted rubber band stores potential (elastic) energy. As the toy flies up from P to Q it gains height (potential energy), moves (kinetic energy) and makes a loud sound (sound energy). So: potential energy → kinetic + potential + sound energy.

Question 15

2 marks

Which of the following explains why the toy flew lower from R to S?

A   Gravitational force acts on the toy only from R to S.
B   There is less kinetic energy in the toy than before.
C   There is less potential energy in the rubber band than before.

  1. A only
  2. A and B only
  3. B and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 3. As the toy flies, energy is gradually converted to heat and sound, so it has less kinetic energy (B) and the rubber band has unwound, storing less potential energy (C). Gravity acts on the toy throughout its flight, not only from R to S, so A is false. The answer is B and C only.

Question 16

2 marks

Which of the following organ systems are involved when we walk and run?

A   Skeletal system
B   Respiratory system
C   Circulatory system
D   Muscular system

  1. A and C only
  2. B and D only
  3. B, C and D only
  4. A, B, C and D

Answer

Option 4. Walking and running need the muscular and skeletal systems to move the body, the respiratory system to take in oxygen, and the circulatory system to deliver oxygen and energy to the muscles. All four systems (A, B, C and D) work together.

Question 17

2 marks

Study the flowchart below carefully.

Which of the following correctly represents X, Y and Z?

XYZ
(1)frictional forceelastic spring forcegravitational force
(2)magnetic forcefrictional forceelastic spring force
(3)elastic spring forcemagnetic forcefrictional force
(4)frictional forcemagnetic forcegravitational force

Answer

Option 4. X does not act from a distance → it is frictional force (needs contact). Y acts from a distance but not only as a pull → magnetic force (can push or pull). Z acts from a distance and only pulls → gravitational force. So X = frictional, Y = magnetic, Z = gravitational.

Question 18

2 marks

Emma set up an experiment as shown in the diagram below.

She hung different number of weights on the spring and measured the length of the spring. Then, she plotted a graph below to show the results.

Using her results, she calculated the extension of the spring. Extension = New length of spring − original length of spring.

If the extension of the spring is 4 cm, how many weights did she use?

  1. 1
  2. 2
  3. 3
  4. 0

Answer

Option 2. The original length (0 weights) is 2 cm. An extension of 4 cm means the new length is 2 + 4 = 6 cm. From the graph, a length of 6 cm corresponds to 2 weights.

Question 19

2 marks

Jeremy set up a circuit as shown in the diagram below.

He placed different objects, J, K, L and M at positions A and B to connect the circuit and recorded if the bulb lights up in the table below.

Position APosition BDid the bulb light up?
JKNo
JLYes
LMYes

Which of the following are likely the materials that objects J, K, L and M are made of?

JKLM
(1)steelrubberironaluminium
(2)woodironrubbersteel
(3)ironaluminiumsteelrubber
(4)rubberwoodaluminiumsteel

Answer

Option 1. J+L and L+M both light up, so J, L and M must all be conductors (metals). J+K does not light up, so K is a non-conductor (insulator). In option 1, J = steel, L = iron and M = aluminium are all metals (conductors) while K = rubber is an insulator — matching every result.

Question 20

2 marks

Kai set up the following circuits using similar bulbs and batteries as shown in the diagram below. All the bulbs and batteries are in good working condition.

He arranged the bulbs from dimmest to brightest. Which of the following has been arranged correctly?

Dimmest→→Brightest
(1)HGEF
(2)FEGH
(3)EGFH
(4)GHFE

Answer

Option 2. Bulbs in series share the battery voltage so they are dimmer, while bulbs in parallel each receive the full voltage and are brighter; more batteries also make bulbs brighter. Ordering the four circuits from the most-shared (dimmest) to the least-shared/most-powered (brightest) gives F, E, G, H.

Question 21

2 marks

Which actions below help to conserve electricity?

A   Use the fan instead of air-conditioner.
B   Switch off the lights when leaving the room.
C   Shower without the water heater on a hot day.
D   Use the half flush instead of full flush for liquid waste.

  1. A and B only
  2. C and D only
  3. A, B and C only
  4. B, C and D only

Answer

Option 3. Using a fan instead of an air-conditioner (A), switching off lights (B) and showering without the water heater (C) all use less electricity. Using the half flush (D) saves water, not electricity. So the answer is A, B and C only.

Question 22

2 marks

Manfred conducted an experiment using the set-up consisting of a torch, an object, a screen and a light sensor, as shown in the diagram below.

He attached the light sensor on top of the object to measure the amount of light received. Then, he repeatedly moved the position of only one item in the set-up before recording the light sensor readings in the table below.

Height of shadow (cm)Light sensor reading (units)
First reading10040
Second reading12540
Third reading14040
Fourth reading16540

What change did Manfred make?

  1. He moved the torch nearer to the object.
  2. He moved the screen nearer to the object.
  3. He moved the object further away from the screen.
  4. He moved the screen further away from the object.

Answer

Option 4. The shadow on the screen grew larger (100 → 165 cm) while the light reaching the sensor on the object stayed the same (40 units). The light at the object is unchanged only if the torch and object are not moved. Moving the screen further from the object makes the shadow bigger without changing the light at the object, so the answer is option 4.

Question 23

2 marks

Kit filled four identical containers, W, X, Y and Z, with water as shown in the diagram below.

Which containers of water have the least and most amount of heat?

Least amount of heatMost amount of heat
(1)ZY
(2)XZ
(3)ZW
(4)YX

Answer

Option 3. The amount of heat depends on both temperature and the amount of water. W has a large amount of water at 95°C, so it has the most heat. Z has a small amount of water at 40°C, so it has the least heat. The answer is least = Z, most = W.

Question 24

2 marks

Chloe set up an experiment using four similar-sized rods made of different materials, P, Q, R and S. She placed the same amount of wax on top of each rod and lowered each rod into a container. Then, she poured water into the container as shown in the diagram below.

Chloe recorded the time taken for all the wax on rods P, Q, R and S, to melt in the table below.

MaterialsTime taken for the wax to melt (minutes)
P5
Q3
R6
S7

Which material, P, Q, R or S, should Chloe use to make a box to store ice cubes so that the ice cubes will melt the slowest?

  1. P
  2. Q
  3. R
  4. S

Answer

Option 4. The slower the wax melts, the poorer the material is at conducting heat. S took the longest time (7 minutes), so it is the poorest conductor of heat. A poor conductor keeps heat from reaching the ice, so the ice melts the slowest. The answer is S.

Question 25

2 marks

Ray lowered an empty glass with a small wooden ball into a container of water until it touched the bottom of the container. The small wooden ball floated as shown in the diagram below.

Which one of the following statements correctly explains the difference in the water level inside and outside the glass?

  1. The air trapped in the glass occupied space.
  2. The small wooden ball in the glass occupied space.
  3. The air trapped in the glass does not have a fixed shape.
  4. The small wooden ball pushed the water out from the glass.

Answer

Option 1. When the glass is pushed down, air is trapped inside it. The trapped air occupies space and pushes the water down, so the water level inside the glass is lower than outside. The wooden ball is too small to cause this difference, so option 1 is correct.

Question 26

2 marks

Three identical metal containers were each filled with substances P, Q and R respectively. A rubber lid was placed at the opening of the three containers. A 1 kg weight was then placed on the rubber lid of each container. Only the rubber lid on the container filled with substance Q moved downwards, as shown in the diagram below.

Which of the following statements correctly explained this observation?

  1. Substances P, Q and R are liquids.
  2. Substance Q has no definite volume.
  3. Substance R is a gas and can be compressed.
  4. Substance P is a solid and Q and R are both gases.

Answer

Option 2. Only Q's lid moved down, so only Q could be compressed — Q is a gas, which has no definite volume and can be squeezed into a smaller space. P and R did not compress, so they are not gases. The correct statement is that substance Q has no definite volume.

Question 27

2 marks

Ali wanted to find out which of the three metal bars, AB, CD and EF, are magnets. He hung each bar from a string and brought them near to each other before releasing them. His results are shown below.

Which of the following is correct?

ABCDEF
(1)magnetic objectmagnetic objectmagnet
(2)magnetmagnetic objectmagnet
(3)magnetmagnetmagnetic object
(4)magnetmagnetmagnet

Answer

Option 3. Two magnets can repel as well as attract; a magnet and a magnetic object can only attract. From the results, AB and CD show behaviour (repulsion possible) that only two magnets can produce, so AB and CD are magnets, while EF only ever attracts, making it a magnetic object. The answer is AB = magnet, CD = magnet, EF = magnetic object.

Question 28

2 marks

The diagram below shows a bar magnet repelled by an electromagnet when two batteries are used. A pointer attached to the spring moves when the spring stretches or compresses.

Which one of the following is correct when only one battery is used?

movement of pointerstrength of electromagnet
(1)upwardsincreased
(2)upwardsdecreased
(3)downwardsincreased
(4)downwardsdecreased

Answer

Option 4. Using fewer batteries (one instead of two) makes the electromagnet weaker, so its strength is decreased. With a weaker repelling force, the bar magnet is pushed up less, the spring is less compressed, and the pointer moves downwards. The answer is downwards and decreased.

Booklet B — Open-Ended (44 marks)

Question 29

4 marks

The diagram below shows a pond habitat.

Animals W and X lay eggs among the stems of plant S.

(a) State one advantage of laying eggs among the stems. [1]

During periods of hot weather, the population of plant R increases at a faster rate and they cover the entire surface of the pond.

(b) Explain why plant S is more affected than plant Q when the population of plant R increased. [2]

Animal X has moist skin while animal W has scales.

(c)(i) Identify the animal groups that X and W belong to. [1]

(c)(ii) Based on the animal groups in (c)(i), give a reason why animal X is less affected than animal W after plants S and T are removed. [1]

Answer

(a) The stems protect/shelter the eggs from predators (or from the rain/sun), and when the eggs hatch the young have a ready source of food nearby.
(b) Plant R covers the surface of the pond and blocks sunlight from reaching plant S (which is underwater). Plant S therefore traps less light and makes less food, while plant Q grows above the water surface and is not blocked, so S is more affected than Q.
(c)(i) X (moist skin): Amphibian.   W (scales): Fish.
(c)(ii) When plants S and T are removed there is less oxygen, food or shelter in the water. Animal X is an amphibian and can move onto land to get oxygen, food or shelter, but animal W is a fish and cannot leave the water, so X is less affected.

Question 30

3 marks

The food web below shows the relationships between organisms P, Q, R, S and T.

(a) Classify organisms P, Q, R, S and T under the correct headings in the table below. [2]

There was a sudden decrease in the population of organism R even though the population of the other organisms remained the same.

(b) Give a possible reason for this decrease. [1]

Answer

(a)
ProducerPlant-eaterAnimal-eater
TQ, PS, R

T is at the start of the web (eaten by others) so it is the producer. Q and P feed directly on the producer (plant-eaters). S and R feed on other animals (animal-eaters).

(b) A new predator of R may have appeared, or an outbreak of disease infected and killed many R, or R migrated away — any of these would reduce R's population while the others stay the same.

Question 31

4 marks

Female animal F lays its eggs on green leaves. Male animal F will guard the eggs after they are laid. Male animal F has spots on its body that look like its eggs and has strong muscular legs. It was also observed that insect W feeds on the eggs of animal F.

(a) Explain how the spots on animal F's body and muscular legs increases the number of eggs hatching into young. [2]

It was observed that another insect Z lays its eggs among animal F's eggs. The larvae of insect Z feeds on the unhatched eggs of animal F. Study graph P and graph Q below.

(b) Which graph, P or Q, correctly shows the duration of the egg stages of animals F and Z such that the larvae of Z has more food. Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

(a) Insect W is tricked/confused into mistaking the spots on male F's body for the eggs. When W tries to eat the spots (or comes close to the eggs), male F uses its strong muscular legs to kick W away. Fewer eggs are eaten, so more eggs survive and hatch into young.
(b) Graph Q. In Graph Q the egg stage of Z is shorter than that of F, so Z's eggs hatch into larvae before F's eggs hatch. The larvae of Z can then feed on F's still-unhatched eggs, giving the larvae more food.

Question 32

3 marks

Tom set up an experiment using three similar leaves, P, Q and R, on the same plant. These leaves have tiny openings on both their upper and lower surfaces. Leaves lose water as water vapour through these tiny openings.

(a) Other than for losing water, state another function of the tiny openings on a leaf. [1]

He coated some surfaces of the leaves with oil as shown in the table below.

LeafUpper surface (coated with oil)Lower surface (coated with oil)
Pyesno
Qnoyes
Rnono

The plant was placed under bright sunlight for two hours. Tom measured the volume of water loss at regular time intervals. His results are shown in the graph below.

Tom concluded that there are more tiny openings on the upper surface than the lower surface of the leaves of this plant.

(b) With reference to the data in the graph and table, explain how he arrived at this conclusion. [2]

Answer

(a) The tiny openings carry out gaseous exchange — they let the leaf take in carbon dioxide and give out oxygen (and take in oxygen / give out carbon dioxide).
(b) Leaf P had its upper surface coated, so it only lost water through its lower surface; leaf Q had its lower surface coated, so it only lost water through its upper surface. From the graph, leaf Q lost more water than leaf P. This shows that more water (and therefore more openings) escapes through the upper surface than the lower surface.

Question 33

2 marks

Study the diagram below.

(a) Identify a gas which is at a higher amount in the blood at K than L. [1]

(b) Other than the part given in the diagram, state all the other part(s) of the circulatory system. [1]

Answer

(a) Carbon dioxide. Blood at K is travelling from the heart to the lungs and carries more carbon dioxide; at L it returns to the heart after the lungs have removed carbon dioxide.
(b) Blood and blood vessels (veins, arteries and capillaries). The diagram already shows the heart, so the other parts of the circulatory system are the blood and the blood vessels.

Question 34

3 marks

Study the diagrams below.

(a) Describe how the circulatory system works together with the digestive system. [2]

John accidentally swallowed a piece of meat without chewing.

(b) Explain why it would take a longer time for his body to digest the meat. [1]

Answer

(a) The digestive system breaks down food into simpler substances, which are absorbed into the blood at the small intestine. The circulatory system then transports this blood, carrying the digested food, to all the different parts of the body.
(b) Because the meat was not chewed, it stays in large pieces. There is less (exposed) surface area of meat in contact with the digestive juices, so it takes a longer time to be broken down.

Question 35

5 marks

The diagram below shows a toy boat with a sail. When Ben turned on the fan at the lowest speed, the wind from the fan caused the toy boat to move forward from point X to point Y.

At point Y, the fan was switched off, but the toy boat continued to move forward before stopping at point Z.

(a) State the source of energy present that caused the toy boat to move. [1]

(b)(i) In terms of energy, give a reason why the toy boat continued to move forward from point Y to point Z. [1]

(b)(ii) In terms of energy, explain why the toy boat stopped moving at point Z. [1]

(c) Using the same equipment in the setup, suggest one way to make the toy boat move a longer distance. Give a reason for your answer in terms of energy. [2]

Answer

(a) The wind / moving air from the fan (the fan is the source of energy).
(b)(i) Even after the fan is switched off, the toy boat still has (some) kinetic energy, so it keeps moving forward.
(b)(ii) By point Z, all the kinetic energy has been converted to heat and sound energy (through friction), so the boat has no kinetic energy left and stops.
(c) Suggestion: Increase the fan speed (or place the boat nearer the fan / open the sail wider). Reason: This gives the boat more kinetic energy, so it can move a longer distance before stopping.

Question 36

4 marks

Trisha conducted an experiment with three similar ramps X, Y and Z as shown in the diagram below.

She placed different numbers of strips of the same length and thickness across ramps Y and Z. She released a ball from the top of the ramp and recorded the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp. The readings are recorded in the table below.

Time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp (seconds)
Ramp X2
Ramp Y5
Ramp Z8

(a) What is the relationship between the number of strips and the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp? [1]

(b) Identify the two forces acting on the ball as it rolls down the ramp. [1]

The diagram below shows a slope at the side of Trisha's house.

(c) Based on the results above, give a reason in terms of forces, how placing more strips on the slope will prevent Trisha from slipping on a rainy day. [1]

(d) How can Trisha increase the reliability of the results of her experiment? [1]

Answer

(a) As the number of strips increases, the time taken for the ball to reach the bottom of the ramp increases.
(b) Friction (frictional force) and gravity (gravitational force).
(c) Placing more strips increases the friction between Trisha's feet and the slope, which prevents her from slipping.
(d) Repeat the experiment at least 3 times for each ramp and calculate the average (or check that the results are consistent).

Question 37

3 marks

Mdm Tan set up a circuit using two identical bulbs, P and Q, and three identical batteries as shown in the diagram below. All the bulbs and batteries are in good working conditions. The metal rod can swing freely about the pivot.

The diagram above shows the metal rod touching point B.

(a) Put a tick in the box below to indicate the expected observation. [1]

When Mdm Tan pressed the button down, the metal spring will be compressed and the metal rod will move up and touch point A.

(b) State and explain the change in her observation for each bulb when she pressed the button. [2]

Answer

(a) When the rod touches B, the lower circuit (bulb P) is complete and the upper circuit (bulb Q) is open:
YesNo
Bulb P✔
Bulb Q✔
(b)(i) Bulb P — Change: it becomes brighter. Explain: touching A puts more batteries into bulb P's circuit, so there is more electric current flowing through it.
(b)(ii) Bulb Q — Change: it lights up. Explain: moving the rod to A completes bulb Q's circuit (a closed circuit), so electric current can now flow through it.

Question 38

4 marks

Evangeline has three puppets, X, Y and Z, of the same height as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Which property of light enables us to see the three puppets? [1]

She placed the three puppets between a torch and a screen and the shadows of the puppets cast on the screen are as follows.

(b) How are shadows formed? [1]

(c) Based on the shadows cast on the screen above, which puppet, X, Y or Z, was placed furthest from the torch? Give a reason for your answer. [1]

(d) Without changing the positions of the three puppets, suggest one change that can be made to increase the size of all the three shadows cast on the screen. [1]

Answer

(a) Light can be reflected. Light reflects off the puppets into our eyes, allowing us to see them.
(b) Shadows are formed when light is blocked by an opaque object.
(c) Puppet Y. Its shadow is the smallest, so it was placed furthest from the torch (the further an object is from the light source, the smaller its shadow).
(d) Move the torch nearer to the puppets (or move the screen further away from the puppets).

Question 39

5 marks

Jim magnetised steel bar XY using the 'stroke' method. The poles of the magnetised steel bar XY is as shown below.

(a) State one property of steel bar XY that allows it to be magnetised. [1]

He magnetised another steel bar CD using two magnets as shown below.

(b) Using information from part (a), state the poles of magnet CD. [1]

Jim wanted to magnetise steel bar EF using the South poles of two magnets as shown below.

(c) Explain using the properties of magnets why steel bar EF cannot be magnetised using the method above. [2]

Answer

(a) It is magnetic (made of a magnetic material such as steel that can be magnetised).
(b) At C: South.   At D: North. (The pole of the steel bar formed next to a magnet's pole is the opposite pole, following the same pattern as in part (a).)
(c) Using two South poles would make both ends E and F become the same pole (both North). A magnet must always have two different poles — one North and one South. Since EF cannot have two like poles, it cannot be properly magnetised this way.

Question 40

4 marks

Ethan set up the experiment as shown in the diagram below.

He placed the same amount of ice cubes into each of the three containers and measured the time taken for all the ice to melt completely. He recorded the readings in the table below.

MaterialTime taken for all the ice cubes to melt (min)
F35
G25
H40

(a) What was the aim of his experiment? [1]

(b) Give a reason why Ethan used the same number of ice cubes in the three set-ups. [1]

Ethan wants to bring some hot chicken soup to his mother's house.

(c) Based on the results above, explain which material, F, G or H, is most suitable for making a container to keep the chicken soup hot for a longer time. [2]

Answer

(a) To find out how the different materials affect the time taken for the ice cubes to melt completely (i.e. to find out which material is the best/poorest conductor of heat).
(b) So that the result (the melting time) is only caused by the different materials used and not by a different amount of ice — this makes it a fair test.
(c) Material H. The ice cubes in container H took the longest time (40 min) to melt, so H is the poorest conductor of heat. It conducts heat from the hot soup to the surrounding air the slowest, so it keeps the soup hot for a longer time.
